head: post-A4

findings.title: cluster_trace MCP arm leaks actorId + entity name/kind through node labels and metadata — sibling-of-cluster_resolve missed by Wave A4 sanitization sweep
findings.leak_or_violation: Wave A4 closed AGG-001 / Surface-B fixes by adding 5-arm sanitization for cluster_resolve (artifact + canonical + receipt + ledger + index). The exact sibling MCP arm cluster_trace was missed. For a kernel constructed WITHOUT policies (the ~614 baseline tests path, which is what MCP server defaults to when DB_CLUSTER_POLICIES_FILE is unset), cluster_trace returns the raw ProvenanceGraph. Every entity node carries label='Kind: name' verbatim (entity.name may be email/PII). Every ledger event node carries label='action by actorId' — the actor identity that sanitizeProvenanceEventForOutput is explicitly designed to strip ('[REDACTED]') at the cluster_resolve boundary. Every receipt node carries label='Receipt: resultSummary' where resultSummary='Created entity: User/john@example.com' (the leak that AGG-006 fixed for the commit/compensate arms). Same trust gradient as cluster_resolve, same fix-shape missed.
findings.recommendation: Add a sanitization pass at src/mcp/server.ts cluster_trace arm: walk graph.nodes, strip metadata.actorId and replace label with redacted form when no policy-enforcement is in place. Alternatively, push sanitization into SDK.traceObject so the SDK boundary handles it unconditionally — symmetric to the AGG-002 fix at sdk/cluster-sdk.ts:238-260. The PolicyEnforcedKernel.traceObject already does this when policies are configured (kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts:491-511 via redactProvenanceActors + redactGraphNodes); the unconditional baseline is what's missing.
findings.family_check: Checked all MCP arms returning store-derived data. cluster_resolve (5-arm sanitization — covered by Wave A4). cluster_find_sources (LIST arm sanitization — covered by Wave A4 SURFACE-B-001). cluster_list_receipts (sanitizeReceiptForOutput on each — covered). cluster_retrieve_bundle (indexRecords / provenanceEvents are NOT spread into response — safe). cluster_trace (THIS FINDING — raw graph spread). cluster_why (also calls traceObject and uses focal.label / creationEdge.reason — same root cause).

findings.title: cluster_why MCP arm leaks entity name/kind + actorId via focal.label and creationEdge.reason — same root cause as V2-A4-001
findings.leak_or_violation: The why() helper constructs a human-readable string from graph nodes that themselves carry owner-truth (entity.kind/name, receipt.resultSummary). The string is returned as `explanation` and surfaced raw across the MCP boundary. PolicyEnforcedKernel.why is also passthrough (kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts:513-515 just enforces trace_provenance + delegates) — so even with policies configured, the resulting STRING contains entity content that the per-node redactor was never applied to (the redactor runs on the GRAPH; why() collapses the graph into a string AFTER the redactor would have run — but PolicyEnforcedKernel.why doesn't call redactor at all on the result).
findings.recommendation: Two options. (a) Replace why() output with a structured object the MCP can sanitize per field. (b) Have PolicyEnforcedKernel.why call traceObject (which already redacts), then construct the explanation string from the REDACTED graph — currently PolicyEnforcedKernel.why bypasses its own redaction by delegating to the raw kernel. The unconditional baseline (no-policy MCP) needs the same fix as V2-A4-001 — strip entity names + actorIds from the rendered text.
findings.family_check: Same family as V2-A4-001 — Wave A4's cluster_resolve sanitization pattern did not propagate to cluster_trace and cluster_why. These two MCP arms share the trace-graph data plane and must share its sanitization.

findings.title: ImportConflictError and LedgerCycleDetectedError extend Error (not ClusterError) — redactError collapses their stable codes to INTERNAL_ERROR and may leak serialized record content
findings.leak_or_violation: Two distinct cross-boundary problems at the kernel→MCP arm. (a) Code-collapse: every adapter-side typed error (ImportConflictError, LedgerCycleDetectedError, CorruptStoreError, InvalidContentHashError) and every URI/resolver error (ClusterUriError, ResolveError) collapses to 'INTERNAL_ERROR' on the MCP boundary, defeating the stable-code-for-host-branching design that Wave A4 wired into redactError for ClusterError subclasses only. (b) Message content: ImportConflictError.message embeds `existingHash`/`incomingHash` which are 120-char-truncated JSON serializations of the conflicting records (entity attributes, ledger event detail, receipt resultSummary — all the owner-truth content the sibling sanitizers exist to strip). Today this error is only thrown from `restore()` which is CLI-only and not exposed through MCP. But (a) the family pattern is wrong, and (b) any future MCP `cluster_restore` / `cluster_import` arm would leak record content through the error catch unless redactError grows a special case. LedgerCycleDetectedError.message contains the visited event-id chain — operator-internal but not catastrophic.
findings.recommendation: Either (preferred) make the adapter typed errors extend ClusterError with stable codes (IMPORT_CONFLICT, LEDGER_CYCLE_DETECTED, CORRUPT_STORE, INVALID_CONTENT_HASH, RESOLVE_NOT_FOUND, INVALID_CLUSTER_URI), or extend redactError's BUILTIN_ERROR_CODES map to include the adapter error class names. The serialized-record-in-message issue should also be addressed: ImportConflictError should carry the hashes as STRUCTURED fields (not stringified into .message) so a future MCP catch arm can include the hash codes (which are already opaque) without the surrounding JSON body. The existing `existingHash`/`incomingHash` field names are misleading — they're full JSON serializations, not hashes.
findings.family_check: Confirmed via grep: 6 typed errors in src/adapters/local/errors.ts + src/uri/cluster-uri.ts + src/resolver/cluster-resolver.ts all extend Error directly. Only the 7 kernel errors in src/kernel/errors.ts extend ClusterError. The redactError ClusterError-first design relies on every typed cross-boundary error being a ClusterError — Wave A4's new types (ContentHashMismatchError, StagedContentTamperedError, CommandQueuePersistenceLostError, BufferSideChannelNotSupportedError) DO extend ClusterError; the adapter family was not promoted.

findings.title: Buffer-side-channel staging tmp files at .db-cluster/pending-content/{hash}.{pid}-{rand16}.tmp have NO orphan sweep — staging-bytes pile up after mid-write crashes
findings.leak_or_violation: Information-flow shape: a propose() that crashes after writeFileSync but before renameSync leaves a `${hash}.${pid}-${rand16}.tmp` file in `.db-cluster/pending-content/`. The adapter-side sweepContentDirOrphans regex requires 1-6 char random suffix — kernel uses 16-char `randomBytes(8).toString('hex')`. Even if the kernel called sweepContentDirOrphans against pending-content/ (which it doesn't), the regex would not match. Result: caller-supplied artifact bytes pile up indefinitely in `.db-cluster/pending-content/*.tmp`. If `.db-cluster/` is then backed up (filesystem-level rsync — the common operator pattern, NOT the JSON-export `backup()` which only walks stores), these tmp files travel across whatever trust boundary the backup crosses. Same shape applies to fully-staged orphans: a propose() that succeeds but is never committed or rejected (process crash, operator forgets) leaves `.db-cluster/pending-content/{hash}` permanently. Wave A4 wired tmp cleanup for the four store-adapter files but not for the new kernel staging directory.
findings.recommendation: Two parts. (a) Align tmp-suffix length OR widen the sweep regex: change kernel's randomBytes(8) to randomBytes(3) (6 hex chars matches the existing regex), OR widen tmp-cleanup.ts content-dir regex to `[a-z0-9]{1,16}` (and apply the same widening to the named-file pattern in cleanupOrphanTmpFiles). (b) Call sweepContentDirOrphans(this.getStagingDir()) in the kernel constructor (or on first getStagingDir() call) so staged orphans are reaped. Additionally consider a CommandQueue→staging consistency sweep: any staging file whose hash is NOT in the queue's known contentHashes is an orphan and can be reaped after maxAgeMs.
findings.family_check: Confirmed: 4 adapter files call cleanupOrphanTmpFiles, 1 calls sweepContentDirOrphans. The kernel's getStagingDir() just mkdirSync's — no sweep. The wave-a4-stores-regression test cohort covers adapter tmp cleanup; no test asserts kernel staging cleanup.

findings.title: CommandQueue marker file is NOT included in backup()/restore() — restore from JSON backup with pending commands recreates marker correctly but a downgraded restore (pre-A4 backup with no marker) hits the silent-empty-queue path
findings.leak_or_violation: Two boundary inconsistencies. (a) backup-then-restore round-trip for a queue with zero pending commands: `data.commands` is `[]`, the for-loop in restore doesn't run, no persist() is called, no marker is created. A restored cluster with zero pending commands lacks the marker; later when an operator hand-deletes `pending-commands.json` for any reason, the cold-start path activates SILENTLY (no persistence-lost error). The CommandQueuePersistenceLostError is supposed to be the typed signal for 'someone deleted my pending queue' — that signal is lost across an empty-queue backup/restore. (b) Cross-boundary information-flow: the marker is a CLAIM that 'this queue has held persisted state.' A backup that omits the marker (because list() returned empty) drops this claim. A restore into a fresh `.db-cluster/` then has a queue that LIES about its history. If the operator later inspects the cluster and sees no marker + no queue, they conclude 'this queue has never held anything,' but the truth is 'this queue was restored from a backup that EXISTED but happened to have zero pending.' The marker is operator-facing forensic state, not just internal bookkeeping.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) backup the marker explicitly (add `hadPendingState: boolean` to ClusterBackup, set from `existsSync(this.markerPath)` at backup time; in restore, write the marker if hadPendingState was true) — preserves the forensic claim. Or (b) document the limitation: 'an empty-queue backup cannot reconstruct the marker; a CommandQueuePersistenceLostError after such a restore is not possible.' Option (a) is the safer fix because it preserves the typed-signal design across the backup boundary.
findings.family_check: Probed the symmetric question for the BufferSideChannel staging dir: it's NOT in backup() at all (backup walks stores, not .db-cluster/), so the staging dir falls out of the JSON-backup boundary entirely. Filesystem-level backups (rsync) carry both marker + pending-content + queue.json together — those boundaries are consistent. The CONFLICT is specifically JSON backup() / restore() where the marker is dropped.

findings.title: redactError does not walk cause chain — secondaryError attached by recordOrphanMutation is never surfaced; nested ledger-failure paths return only top-level message
findings.leak_or_violation: The design choice to not walk the cause chain (sanitize.ts:163-168) is correct for the leak-prevention goal, BUT ReceiptFailedError's CONSTRUCTOR already inlines `cause.message` into the outer .message — so the SAME leak path the no-walk rule was meant to block is open via direct concatenation. Example: a ledger write fails with `Error('ENOENT: open /home/user/.db-cluster/ledger/events.json')`. The orphan-write also fails (so secondaryError is attached). ReceiptFailedError.message is `Mutation succeeded but receipt/provenance emission failed for subject xyz (command abc): ENOENT: open /home/user/.db-cluster/ledger/events.json`. The PATH_REGEX would scrub this — OK. BUT: a downstream operator inspecting the error via DEBUG mode (sanitize.ts:171,177-178) gets BOTH the scrubbed and raw messages appended. The DEBUG path is gated on `process.env.DEBUG === '1'` which an MCP host running in a less-trusted context might still set. More importantly: the `secondaryError` field attached by recordOrphanMutation is NEVER reachable through the MCP boundary at all — it's silently swallowed. Operators get the primary failure signal but no signal that the orphan ALSO failed, defeating the dual-prong KERNEL-R009 design.
findings.recommendation: Two fixes. (a) Add a stable structured field to RedactedError for secondaryError signaling: `{code, message, secondaryCode?}` where secondaryCode is the code of the attached secondary error (without its message — just the stable code). This preserves the 'don't walk cause' invariant while giving the host the signal that a secondary failure occurred. (b) Remove `cause.message` concatenation from ReceiptFailedError.message and instead surface the cause as a separate field on the Error instance (e.g., `causeCode`). The current concatenation pattern bypasses the no-walk rule because the cause is already inlined before redactError ever sees the error.
findings.family_check: Checked all 4 new Wave A4 typed errors: ContentHashMismatchError (no cause inline, OK), StagedContentTamperedError (no cause inline, OK), CommandQueuePersistenceLostError (no cause inline, OK), BufferSideChannelNotSupportedError (no cause inline, OK). The pre-existing CommandQueueCorruptError DOES inline `causeMsg` into .message (errors.ts:81-83) — same pattern as ReceiptFailedError. Two kernel errors share this anti-pattern.

findings.title: PATH_REGEX does not scrub bare-Posix paths embedded inside JSON strings (paths inside JSON-encoded error payloads survive the boundary)
findings.leak_or_violation: PATH_REGEX is comprehensive for most direct path leaks but has narrow gaps. The most concerning is RELATIVE paths: `.db-cluster/pending-content/abc123` (used in StagedContentTamperedError.message). The regex requires an absolute leading anchor (`[A-Za-z]:`, `\\`, or `/`). A relative path like `.db-cluster/pending-content/{hash}` would NOT match. Looking at the actual StagedContentTamperedError construction (kernel/errors.ts:166-178): `stagingPath` is a string passed in by the kernel via `join(stagingDir, contentHash)` — `stagingDir` is `join(this.dataDir, 'pending-content')` where `this.dataDir` is whatever the kernel was constructed with. If the kernel is constructed with a RELATIVE dataDir (e.g., `'.db-cluster'`), the stagingPath is relative and bypasses PATH_REGEX. Default kernel construction in MCP server uses `resolve(process.env.DB_CLUSTER_DIR ?? process.cwd(), '.db-cluster')` (server.ts:24) which is absolute — OK. But CLI constructs with `resolve(process.cwd(), '.db-cluster')` — also absolute. So in PRACTICE all production paths are absolute. The gap is theoretical for testing/dev mode where the kernel is constructed with a relative dataDir.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) document that all kernel staging paths MUST be absolute (already true in practice — assert it at the boundary), or (b) widen PATH_REGEX to include a `\.db-cluster/` anchor + relative pattern. Low priority — the production path is safe.
findings.family_check: Tested: PATH_REGEX against Windows (covered), UNC paths (covered), Posix abs (covered), Posix relative `./...` (NOT covered — by design, terminating `.` is a terminator). The fix in v1 verifier may flag the same pattern.

findings.title: Adapter-thrown errors (LedgerCycleDetectedError, CorruptStoreError) bypass kernel error wrapping and surface as raw INTERNAL_ERROR through MCP cluster_trace boundary
findings.leak_or_violation: When a cluster_trace MCP call hits a corrupt ledger (cycle in parentEventId chain), the adapter throws LedgerCycleDetectedError. Neither the kernel nor the SDK wraps this in a ClusterError. At the MCP boundary, redactError sees `instanceof Error` (the generic branch) and assigns code='INTERNAL_ERROR'. The host loses the stable 'LEDGER_CYCLE_DETECTED' signal it could use to branch on (e.g., trigger a doctor() check). The error MESSAGE is preserved (`Cycle detected in ledger parent chain. Visited ids in order: ...`) — that's diagnosable. But the stable code that hosts WOULD branch on is lost. Same shape applies to CorruptStoreError thrown from local adapter load() paths — any read can throw it, kernel doesn't translate, MCP shows 'INTERNAL_ERROR'.
findings.recommendation: Either (preferred) make adapter errors extend ClusterError so they get stable codes for free, OR add an instanceof-check + code-mapping layer in the kernel (translate adapter errors into ClusterError subclasses at the kernel→SDK boundary), OR extend redactError's BUILTIN_ERROR_CODES to include `LedgerCycleDetectedError: 'LEDGER_CYCLE_DETECTED', CorruptStoreError: 'CORRUPT_STORE', ImportConflictError: 'IMPORT_CONFLICT', InvalidContentHashError: 'INVALID_CONTENT_HASH', ResolveError: 'RESOLVE_NOT_FOUND', ClusterUriError: 'INVALID_CLUSTER_URI'`. The third option is the smallest change and matches the existing BUILTIN_ERROR_CODES idiom.
findings.family_check: Same root cause as V2-A4-003. The two findings are paired: V2-A4-003 calls out the typed-error hierarchy gap (Error vs ClusterError), V2-A4-008 calls out the resulting code-loss on a specific live cross-MCP path. Listed separately because the recommendation has a code-mapping path (V2-A4-008) and a hierarchy-promotion path (V2-A4-003) — they could land independently.

findings.title: deleteStagingFile and orphan staging files: rejected commands' staging files deleted; CRASHED-mid-propose tmp files (named `${hash}.${pid}-${rand}.tmp`) never deleted by any code path
findings.leak_or_violation: Mid-propose crash window: between writeFileSync(tmpPath, buffer) and renameSync(tmpPath, stagingPath), a process crash leaves the buffer content on disk at `${stagingPath}.${pid}-{rand}.tmp`. Rejection path (deleteStagingFile) only knows about `stagingPath`, not the tmp variant — the tmp content sits in `.db-cluster/pending-content/*.tmp` forever. Same shape as V2-A4-004 but specifically for the tmp window. The information-flow concern: an operator who later inspects `.db-cluster/` (or backs it up to a different trust zone) carries that caller-supplied content with them. Combined with the missing sweep (V2-A4-004), there's no recovery path short of manual cleanup.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) make proposeMutation register the tmpPath in a per-process set + add a process.on('exit', cleanup) handler — fragile, doesn't catch SIGKILL. Or (b) the V2-A4-004 fix (sweep on startup) addresses this same orphan class. Or (c) catch the renameSync failure explicitly and `unlinkSync(tmpPath)` in the finally branch. The cleanest fix is (b) — orphan sweep on kernel construction, which would catch ALL mid-write tmp leftovers regardless of how they got there.
findings.family_check: Paired with V2-A4-004 (the same sweep-missing root cause). Mid-write tmp orphans + never-committed staged-hash orphans are the same family — both need a sweep at the kernel staging dir.

findings.title: Policy dry-run (CLI policy explain/test) evaluates against the SAME policies.json runtime uses, but the kernel construction path additionally injects DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES / DEFAULT_VISIBILITY_RULES when missing — dry-run may show ALLOW where runtime DENIES (or vice versa)
findings.leak_or_violation: Dry-run and runtime DRIFT for policy configs that omit trustZones or visibilityRules. CLI dry-run substitutes DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES when missing; CLI runtime passes `undefined` straight to PolicyEnforcedKernel. PolicyEnforcedKernel may handle `undefined` differently than `DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES` — namely, evaluate-policy.ts's matchTrustZones treats an undefined match.trustZones list as 'no constraint' (allow), while DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES might restrict via cross-zone rules. Result: operator runs `policy explain --principal X --capability Y` and sees a decision DIFFERENT from what the kernel would actually produce. The SURFACE-B-002 fix (Wave A4) wired the dry-run to the same policies.json file the runtime uses, but the fallback substitution introduces drift. The boundary that was supposed to be 'dry-run == runtime' actually has a gap.
findings.recommendation: Make dry-run substitution and runtime substitution SYMMETRIC. Either (a) the runtime ALSO substitutes DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES / DEFAULT_VISIBILITY_RULES when undefined (move the substitution into getKernel()), or (b) the dry-run also passes `undefined` raw when the file omits them. Whichever direction is chosen, both paths must be consistent. Recommend (a) — substituting in getKernel() means the operator config 'just works' for zone-defaults, matching the dry-run's friendlier shape.
findings.family_check: Probed parallel: MCP server runtime also constructs PolicyEnforcedKernel — sdk/cluster-sdk.ts:167-176. Passes `trustZones: options.trustZones` raw too. Same drift shape would apply to MCP cluster_policy_explain vs MCP cluster_propose. But the MCP cluster_policy_explain path goes through sdk.policyExplain (cluster-sdk.ts:330-375) which uses `this.policyOptions` — set at SDK construction with `trustZones: options.trustZones` (also undefined-or-raw). So SDK has internal consistency but MCP dry-run and CLI dry-run differ from each other when trustZones is omitted (SDK passes undefined, CLI substitutes DEFAULTS). Worse: the CLI dry-run and CLI runtime diverge as called out.

findings.title: redactProvenanceActors label-redaction regex `/by\s+[\w\-@.]+/g` does not handle non-ASCII actor IDs (UTF-8 names, emoji handles) — non-ASCII actor names leak through cluster_trace label
findings.leak_or_violation: When PolicyEnforced cluster_trace fires the actor-redaction rule, the regex assumes actorId is ASCII-only. Non-ASCII actor IDs (international names, email-encoded usernames in cyrillic/greek/cjk, emoji handles) escape the redaction. Most production deployments may use ASCII-only actor IDs by convention, but the regex doesn't enforce that — a single non-ASCII actor breaks the redaction silently. Trust gradient violation: a principal with `provenance_actors: strip` policy still sees the actor identity for any non-ASCII actor.
findings.recommendation: Use the `u` flag and `\p{L}` (unicode letter class): `/by\s+[\p{L}\p{N}\-@._]+/gu` — matches letters of any script. Or, more robust: parse the label structure (`<action> by <actor>`) instead of regex-matching, and replace everything after `by ` with the redaction marker up to the next whitespace.
findings.family_check: Probed redactMetadataActors (redactor.ts:209-217): operates on field names (actorId, actor, proposedBy, etc.) — no character-class issue, OK. Probed `[\w\-@.]+` usages across the codebase: this is the only one. So the gap is isolated to label-redaction.

findings.title: MCP cluster_propose_mutation forwards payload across the boundary unsanitized — entity name/kind/attributes IN, command record back OUT carries the same fields raw via formatCommandOutput
findings.leak_or_violation: The MCP caller supplied the payload, so they already know it on the propose round-trip — that side is fine. The concern is cross-actor: actor A proposes a command with sensitive payload (entity name = 'CEO@company.com' attributes = {salary: ...}). Actor B (different principal, different MCP session) later calls cluster_inspect_command with that commandId. formatCommandOutput returns the FULL payload to actor B. PolicyEnforcedKernel.inspectCommand (kernel/policy-enforced-kernel.ts:608-616) DOES enforce read_command + redactCommand — but only when policy-enforcement is in place. The unconditional MCP boundary does NOT sanitize the payload. Wave A4 fixed cluster_resolve / cluster_find_sources / cluster_list_receipts to sanitize unconditionally; cluster_inspect_command was left as a policy-gated path. Inconsistent baseline.
findings.recommendation: Two options. (a) Add a redactCommandPayload sanitizer (src/mcp/sanitize.ts) that strips command.payload entries known to be owner-truth (entity name, attributes, artifact content), keeping verb-essential fields (kind, mimeType, IDs). Call unconditionally from cluster_inspect_command + cluster_propose_mutation arms — keeps the propose round-trip self-consistent. (b) Mark `cluster_inspect_command` as policy-required (refuse to run without policies configured), forcing the policy-enforced redactor to fire. Option (a) preserves the unconditional-baseline pattern that Wave A4 established.
findings.family_check: Probed all 7 lifecycle arms that call formatCommandOutput: propose, validate, approve, reject, inspect, commit, compensate. All return raw payload. The MCP boundary's design (Wave A4 sanitize-unconditionally pattern) is inconsistently applied — store-output objects sanitize, command-payload objects don't. Cross-actor inspect is the realistic risk path.

summary: Wave A4 closed 11 Stage-A regression items and migrated tests to tmpdir, but the canonical sanitization pattern (unconditional MCP-boundary sanitizers + 5-arm coverage) was applied to cluster_resolve, cluster_find_sources, cluster_list_receipts, and the commit/compensate receipt-attach arms — and missed three sibling MCP arms that share the same kernel→MCP boundary: cluster_trace (V2-A4-001), cluster_why (V2-A4-002), and the 7 command-lifecycle arms via formatCommandOutput (V2-A4-012). The trace-builder labels embed actorId and entity name verbatim into ProvenanceGraph nodes, which then cross the MCP boundary raw when no policies are configured. Separately, six typed errors in adapters/local and uri/ resolver still extend Error (not ClusterError), so they collapse to INTERNAL_ERROR on the redactError boundary (V2-A4-003, V2-A4-008) — losing the stable-code-for-host-branching design Wave A4 wired in for ClusterError subclasses only. The new kernel-side staging directory (`.db-cluster/pending-content/`) has no orphan sweep (V2-A4-004) and the tmp-naming convention (16-hex random suffix) doesn't match the existing adapter sweep regex (1-6 hex chars). Mid-propose crash leftovers and never-committed staged orphans accumulate forever (V2-A4-009). The CommandQueue marker is dropped from JSON backup/restore round-trips (V2-A4-005). CLI dry-run / runtime substitution drift remains: dry-run substitutes DEFAULT_TRUST_ZONES on omission, runtime passes undefined raw (V2-A4-010). Lower-severity: redactProvenanceActors regex is ASCII-only (V2-A4-011); ReceiptFailedError inlines cause.message bypassing the no-walk rule (V2-A4-006); PATH_REGEX gaps on relative paths (V2-A4-007).
lens_quality_self_assessment: High confidence on V2-A4-001 / V2-A4-002 / V2-A4-003: read all relevant code, verified label construction in trace-builder, verified redactError code-path branching, confirmed adapter error class hierarchy via grep. V2-A4-004 / V2-A4-009 high confidence on the missing sweep — verified by grep that no caller invokes sweepContentDirOrphans against the kernel staging dir AND that the kernel tmp-suffix (randomBytes(8)=16 hex) doesn't match the existing 1-6-char regex. V2-A4-005 high confidence: read backup.ts in full, traced commandQueue argument shape, confirmed marker creation only fires in persist(). V2-A4-010 medium-high confidence on the substitution drift — verified both CLI paths in cli.ts, MCP/SDK paths in cluster-sdk.ts; the runtime side passes undefined, dry-run substitutes defaults. V2-A4-011 high confidence on ASCII-only regex limitation but unable to confirm a deployed instance uses non-ASCII actor IDs — the lens is correctness-of-redactor, not actual-leak-in-production. V2-A4-006 / V2-A4-007 lower confidence: V2-A4-006's argument that secondaryError is unreachable is concrete, but the recommendation depends on what the consumer protocol is; V2-A4-007's PATH_REGEX gaps are theoretical for the in-tree production paths (all absolute today). V2-A4-008 paired with V2-A4-003 and V2-A4-012 paired with the command-lifecycle gap — listed separately because the fix paths are distinct. The lens scope (boundary-information-flow) caught more than the contract-completeness lens would: trace-node label embeddings and adapter-error class-hierarchy are both boundary-shape problems that pure contract-coverage would miss. Top 3 by impact: V2-A4-001 (cluster_trace actor+name leak — unconditional baseline missed), V2-A4-003 (adapter error class hierarchy — INTERNAL_ERROR collapse + serialized-record-in-message), V2-A4-004 (kernel staging orphan sweep missing — caller-bytes pile up). Biggest blind spot: cross-rig / multi-process consistency on the staging directory was not deeply audited — if two MCP server processes concurrently propose with the SAME contentHash, both would writeFileSync to the same path through the rename-after-tmp pattern; the rename is atomic but the staging file could be silently rewritten between propose and commit on a second propose. That window is V3's invariant-completeness lens (test coverage of multi-writer staging).
